text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 58-year-old Polish-American male, retired steel mill worker, referred for medical oncology consultation following new diagnosis of stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous liver metastases identified on staging workup.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Kowalski initially presented to his primary care physician approximately 3-4 weeks ago with a several-month history of lower abdominal cramping, change in bowel habits (alternating constipation and loose stools), and mild rectal bleeding. He reported approximately 12 lbs of unintentional weight loss over the preceding 3 months. He had not undergone prior colorectal cancer screening. Colonoscopy was performed and demonstrated a circumferential, partially obstructing sigmoid colon mass located 18 cm from the anal verge. Multiple biopsies were taken. Pathology returned invasive moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, CDX2+/CK20+/CK7-. MMR IHC showed retained expression of all four mismatch repair proteins (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2), consistent with proficient MMR/microsatellite stable (pMMR/MSS) tumor.

Staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with IV and oral contrast demonstrated the primary sigmoid mass (4.1 x 3.8 cm) with pericolonic lymphadenopathy up to 1.5 cm. Additionally, four hepatic lesions were identified across bilateral lobes: a dominant right lobe segment VI deposit measuring 3.2 x 2.8 cm, a segment IVb lesion of 1.8 x 1.6 cm, a left lobe segment II lesion of 1.4 x 1.1 cm, and a segment VII lesion of 0.9 x 0.8 cm -- all with imaging characteristics consistent with metastatic deposits. No pulmonary nodules, peritoneal carcinomatosis, or osseous metastases were identified. This represents M1a disease (liver-only metastases at initial staging).

Comprehensive genomic profiling via Foundation One CDx was obtained from the colonic biopsy specimen. Key findings: KRAS exon 2 codon 12 G12D mutation (VAF 42%, pathogenic), TP53 p.R248W (VAF 48%), APC p.E1554* truncating mutation (VAF 51%), and SMAD4 p.R361H (VAF 38%, likely pathogenic, TGF-beta pathway -- potentially adverse prognostic marker in metastatic CRC). CDKN2A homozygous deletion and MYC locus gain also identified. TMB 5 mutations/Mb (low). MSS confirmed. BRAF V600E not detected. NRAS wild-type. HER2 not amplified. No NTRK fusions.

Serum tumor markers at diagnosis: CEA 148.2 ng/mL (markedly elevated), CA 19-9 312 U/mL (elevated). Baseline LFTs mildly elevated, likely reflecting hepatic metastatic burden (ALT 52, AST 48 U/L). Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL. CBC within normal limits. HbA1c 6.8% (diet-controlled diabetes, adequate glycemic control).

The case was brought to the hepatobiliary and colorectal multidisciplinary tumor board for discussion prior to this consultation. See tumor board summary below.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Hypertension -- controlled, on lisinopril 10 mg daily
2. Hyperlipidemia -- on atorvastatin 40 mg daily
3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- diet-controlled; HbA1c 6.8%, no medications required
4. No prior abdominal or pelvic surgeries
5. No prior chemotherapy or radiation

SOCIAL HISTORY
Retired steel mill worker from Pittsburgh, PA. Married, lives with wife. Two adult children. 25 pack-year smoking history -- quit approximately 12 years ago. Moderate alcohol use, approximately 2-3 drinks per week. Denies illicit drug use. BMI 27.4. Has healthcare proxy (wife). Has not completed advance directive at this time.

FAMILY HISTORY
No known family history of colorectal cancer or polyposis syndromes. No Lynch syndrome or FAP in family. Father deceased from myocardial infarction in his 60s. Mother alive, has hypertension. Siblings: one brother with hypertension.

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies. NKDA.

MEDICATIONS
1. Lisinopril 10 mg oral daily (hypertension)
2. Atorvastatin 40 mg oral daily at bedtime (hyperlipidemia)
3. Aspirin 81 mg oral daily (cardiovascular prophylaxis)
4. Multivitamin daily

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Positive for: lower abdominal cramping (improving since colonoscopy), change in bowel habits, unintentional weight loss (~12 lbs over 3 months), mild fatigue, occasional low-grade nausea. Denies: fevers, chills, night sweats, hematemesis, melena (notes intermittent hematochezia, now reduced after colonoscopy). Denies dyspnea, cough, chest pain, palpitations. Denies bone pain, headache, confusion, focal neurologic symptoms. Denies dysuria, hematuria. Psychological: patient reports significant anxiety since diagnosis, support from wife and family. Appetite mildly decreased.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital signs: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, Temperature 98.4°F, Weight 176 lbs (BMI 27.4).
General: Alert, oriented, appears well-nourished and in no acute distress. Mildly anxious affect, appropriate.
HEENT: Anicteric sclerae. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular regions. Oral mucosa moist, no lesions.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze or rhonchi.
Abdomen: Soft. Mild tenderness to deep palpation in the left lower quadrant without guarding or rebound. No peritoneal signs. Liver span approximately 14 cm by percussion (mildly enlarged). No palpable splenic enlargement. No ascites detected clinically.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No clubbing. Distal pulses intact.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 throughout. Sensation grossly intact. Reflexes 2+ and symmetric bilaterally.
Skin: No rashes, no jaundice, no palmar erythema.

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC: WBC 7.8 K/uL (diff: 68% neutrophils, 22% lymphocytes, 8% monocytes), Hgb 13.4 g/dL, Hct 40.1%, Plt 287 K/uL
CMP: Na 138, K 4.0, Cl 102, CO2 24, BUN 14, Cr 0.9 mg/dL (eGFR >60), Glucose 104 mg/dL
LFTs: ALT 52 U/L, AST 48 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 112 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, Albumin 3.8 g/dL
Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1, within normal limits
CEA: 148.2 ng/mL (markedly elevated)
CA 19-9: 312 U/mL (elevated)
HbA1c: 6.8%
Lipid panel: LDL 88 mg/dL (at goal on statin), HDL 44 mg/dL, TG 148 mg/dL
Urinalysis: No protein on dipstick

IMAGING RESULTS
CT CAP with contrast (staging): Four hepatic metastases across bilateral lobes as described. Dominant right lobe segment VI lesion 3.2 x 2.8 cm. Primary sigmoid mass 4.1 x 3.8 cm with N2a pericolonic adenopathy. No pulmonary metastases. No peritoneal disease. No osseous metastases. Potentially resectable hepatic disease, borderline given bilobar involvement.

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD SUMMARY
Case presented at combined hepatobiliary/colorectal MDT. Surgical oncology assessment: Hepatic disease is technically resectable but borderline. The bilobar distribution (segments II, IVb, VI, VII) means that an upfront two-stage resection would be required, and the segment VI lesion may require a formal right hepatectomy; estimated future liver remnant (FLR) with current disease burden would be marginal. Colorectal surgery assessment: Primary sigmoid tumor is asymptomatic; no evidence of impending obstruction on imaging. Colostomy or resection is not immediately required. Consensus decision: Pursue conversion/induction chemotherapy with the intent of downstaging the hepatic disease to improve resectability, reduce extent of hepatic resection required, and evaluate tumor biology before committing to surgery. Systemic oncology recommendation: mFOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab for 4-6 cycles with interval restaging, and repeat tumor board review after approximately 8 cycles to reassess surgical candidacy.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Assessment: Mr. Kowalski is a 58-year-old male with newly diagnosed, biopsy-confirmed stage IV (cT3 N2a M1a, AJCC 8th ed.) moderately differentiated sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous bilobar liver metastases. KRAS G12D mutant, BRAF wild-type, pMMR/MSS. Left-sided primary. CEA markedly elevated at 148.2. ECOG performance status 1. Borderline hepatic resectability. No extrahepatic disease at staging. Goals of therapy are potentially curative in intent if conversion to resectable hepatic disease is achieved.

Key considerations:
- KRAS G12D mutation: Excludes benefit from EGFR-targeted agents (cetuximab, panitumumab). No approved KRAS G12D-directed therapy currently available outside clinical trial.
- pMMR/MSS: Not a candidate for checkpoint immunotherapy.
- BRAF WT, HER2 non-amplified, NTRK fusion-negative: No additional targeted therapy options at this time.
- Left-sided primary with liver-only metastases and BRAF wild-type: Favorable biology for conversion chemotherapy approach.
- SMAD4 mutation identified: Associated with higher risk of peritoneal spread and potentially reduced benefit from hepatic resection in some series -- will factor into surgical planning decisions.

Plan:
1. SYSTEMIC THERAPY: Initiate modified FOLFOX-6 plus bevacizumab (Avastin). Regimen: bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV q14 days; oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours; leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours; 5-fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus; then 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours. Cycle length 14 days. Rationale: FOLFOX/bevacizumab is a well-established first-line backbone for metastatic CRC; bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) adds efficacy in the first-line setting and is appropriate given KRAS mutation precluding anti-EGFR use. Goal is tumor downsizing for potential surgical conversion.

2. ANTIEMETIC PROPHYLAXIS: Ondansetron 8 mg IV pre-chemotherapy; dexamethasone 12 mg IV; palonosetron for delayed nausea. Oral ondansetron and prochlorperazine prescribed for breakthrough nausea at home.

3. VASCULAR ACCESS: Port-a-cath placement arranged with interventional radiology for continuous 5-FU infusion and subsequent cycle administration. Patient to contact IR scheduling today.

4. MONITORING -- BEVACIZUMAB-SPECIFIC TOXICITIES:
   - Blood pressure: Baseline BP today 128/78 mmHg on lisinopril 10 mg. Monitor BP at each visit; hypertension is a class effect of VEGF inhibition. Target <140/90 mmHg; will escalate antihypertensive therapy as needed.
   - Urine protein: Check urine protein:creatinine ratio prior to each cycle; hold bevacizumab for UPC >2.0.
   - Wound healing: No surgery planned imminently; counsel patient to report any wound, GI bleeding, or thromboembolic symptoms promptly.
   - Bowel perforation/fistula: Low risk given no prior abdominal surgery and no peritoneal disease, but patient counseled.

5. MONITORING -- OXALIPLATIN-SPECIFIC TOXICITIES: Counsel extensively on peripheral sensory neuropathy (acute cold-induced dysesthesias and cumulative sensory neuropathy). Advise cold avoidance (gloves, avoiding cold beverages) immediately following infusion. Will perform neuropathy assessment at each visit using NCI CTCAE grading; dose-reduce or discontinue oxaliplatin if Grade 2 or higher persistent neuropathy develops.

6. RESTAGING: Repeat CT CAP after 4 cycles (~8 weeks) for interim response assessment. If significant hepatic response, re-present at MDT after cycle 8. If progression on first restaging, reassess regimen.

7. TUMOR MARKERS: CEA and CA 19-9 to be drawn every 2 cycles. Trending CEA will serve as an adjunct to imaging in monitoring response.

8. DIABETES MANAGEMENT: HbA1c 6.8%, currently diet-controlled. Dexamethasone in premedication regimen may transiently elevate blood glucose -- patient counseled on home glucose monitoring around infusion days. Will coordinate with PCP as needed.

9. CARDIOLOGY/HYPERTENSION: Coordinate with PCP regarding blood pressure management as bevacizumab therapy proceeds.

10. GENETICS/COUNSELING: No family history of CRC; germline Lynch syndrome testing not indicated by current family history and tumor MMR proficiency. No referral placed at this time.

11. FOLLOW-UP: Patient to return in 2 weeks for Cycle 2 Day 1, or sooner if concerning symptoms develop. Emergency oncology contact number provided. Patient and wife both present today; education materials provided regarding chemotherapy regimen, port care, and side effect management.

The patient was counseled at length regarding the goals of therapy (potentially curative intent if hepatic conversion achieved, otherwise disease control and survival prolongation in the palliative setting), realistic expectations for response, and major side effects of the proposed regimen. He expressed understanding and motivation to proceed. He is accompanied by his wife, who is supportive and engaged. Questions answered. Consent obtained for chemotherapy.

Attending Medical Oncologist
Multidisciplinary Gastrointestinal Oncology Program
